core_concepts: Pull Day Structure: Focused on back (lats/rhomboids/traps), biceps, and rear delts.
Feeder Sets: A warm-up protocol using multiple sets with gradually increasing RPE (4-10) to prepare for a heavy all-out set to failure.
Mechanical Tension & Mind-Muscle Connection: Balancing heavy compound loading with isolation work for better fiber recruitment.
Lengthened-Position Hypertrophy: Emphasis on training muscles (especially biceps/back) at their most stretched position, as studies indicate this is highly hypertrophic.
Dropsets: Used at the end of key exercises to push fibers to high levels of activation when technical failure is reached.
Bicep Anatomy Bias: Overhead bicep curl variations can bias the long head (the "peak") compared to standard curls with arms at the side.
Asymmetry Mitigation: Unilateral training (one arm at a time) to identify and correct strength/size imbalances.

actionable_advice.warm_up: 5 minutes light cardio, 10 arm circles per side, 10 cable external rotations per side.
actionable_advice.feeder_set_protocol: 4-5 sets building up: Set 1 (RPE 4-5), Set 2 (RPE 6-7), Set 3 (RPE 7-8), Final Set (RPE 10/Failure).
actionable_advice.exercise_execution: On primary compound back movements, use a consistent grip width across feeder sets to establish a baseline. Use unilateral movements for biceps to prioritize mind-muscle connection and prevent imbalances.
actionable_advice.dropset_method: Once technical failure is reached, strip 30% of the weight and perform 4-5 additional reps.
actionable_advice.bicep_peak: Include at least one overhead bicep curl variation to target the long head of the bicep.

stretched_position_training.description: Training at longer muscle lengths (e.g., preacher curls from 0-50 degrees) produces significantly more hypertrophy than training at shorter lengths.

bicep_long_head_bias.description: Overhead bicep curl variations may bias the long head of the bicep (outer part/peak) compared to arms-down positions.

emg_individual_variation.description: Muscle activation patterns are highly individual, suggesting the need for personal experimentation rather than universal rules.

coaching_insights: Feeder sets are superior to short warm-ups for exercises where mind-muscle connection is a common bottleneck (e.g., lat pulldowns).
Technical failure on back exercises is often premature due to secondary muscles; dropsets allow the back to reach true fatigue.
Consistency in grip width during feeder sets is non-negotiable for strength baseline tracking.
If an exercise feels like you're 'just moving weight,' prioritize a unilateral version to regain focus on the target tissue.
common_mistakes: Excessive grip width variance during warm-up sets, leading to inconsistent strength tracking.
Stopping sets because the bicep/back technically failed, ignoring that the fibers could handle more work via partials or dropsets.
Neglecting unilateral work, allowing strength asymmetries to grow over time.
Over-relying on short, minimal warm-ups for complex movements where neurological priming is needed.

motivational_quotes: Train the length, grow the strength.
Don't just move weight, build muscle.
Strength is a baseline; hypertrophy is an art.
Balance the load, correct the weak links.
The hardest rep is just the beginning of the growth.
Master the connection to master the muscle.
Efficiency is important, but effectiveness is the priority.
Identify the imbalance, rectify the form.
Focus on the stretch, stay for the contraction.

myths_addressed: Feeder sets are just wasted energy.
Full failure is the only way to trigger growth.
Wide grip is always better for lats.
All bicep curls are created equal.
Unilateral training is only for physical therapy.
You don't need to warm up if you're strong.
The 'peak' of the bicep is purely genetic (can't be trained).
The back is just one big muscle.
Heavy weight is the only indicator of a good workout.

grit_application.implementation_strategy: The app should implement a 'Neural Priming & Feeder' mode. 1. For compound lifts, the app provides a guided warm-up flow that prompts the user to increase intensity (RPE 4 to 10) across 4 sets before the main working set. 2. A 'Unilateral Toggle' feature for bicep/back exercises that calculates weight based on the user's weaker side history. 3. A 'Dropset Assist' feature that automatically updates the target weight (30% reduction) once the user logs 'Technical Failure'. 4. A 'Stretch Focus' indicator on exercises where the research supports an emphasis on the lengthened position.
